title: Patience (Savlanut): The Jewish Art of Waiting Without Anger
title_zh: 耐心(Savlanut):犹太人不带愤怒地等待的艺术
title_he: סַבְלָנוּת: האמנות היהודית של להמתין בלי כעס
author: Torah Light Team
chinese_cultural_parallels: Bá miáo zhù zhǎng (拔苗助長) - Pulling up seedlings
Chinese idioms: 心急吃不了热豆腐, 欲速则不达, 磨刀不误砍柴工, 水滴石穿
other_sources: Rabbi Samson Raphael Hirsch - Commentary on Genesis
practical_exercises: Regular Review of Biblical Patience Stories
key_themes: Patience as active strength, not passive resignation
Biblical examples of patience (Abraham, Moses, Joseph)
Divine patience (Erech Apayim) as model for human behavior
Patience in relationships, child-rearing, learning, and spiritual growth
summary: An exploration of the Jewish concept of savlanut (patience) as an active spiritual practice. The article examines biblical narratives of patience (Abraham's 25-year wait, Moses' 40 years in Midian, Joseph's imprisonment), connects to divine patience (Erech Apayim), and provides practical applications in relationships, parenting, Torah study, and spiritual growth. Includes rich Chinese cultural parallels (Wu Wei, agricultural wisdom, Confucian cultivation, the character 忍) and practical exercises for developing patience as a lifelong discipline.
notes: This article emphasizes Chinese cultural parallels throughout, drawing connections between Jewish patience (savlanut) and Daoist/Confucian concepts. Includes Chinese idioms and characters to make the concept relatable to Mandarin-speaking readers. The English version is 1472 words (within the 1200-1500 word range), the Chinese version exceeds 800 characters (922 characters), and includes a full Hebrew translation.
